CURRENT AFFAIRS.

~ The United Irish League authorities are endeavour-
ing to stir up the United Irish Leaguers on the indus-
trial question. We won’t stay to ask wky this was
not vigorously done before; we are glad it is being
done now, and we hope the letter and leaflet which
has been sent to all branches of the organisation will
stir the Leaguers up. Anyway, the sending of the
letter and leaflet is good propagandist work, and it can
hardly fail to have some effect. The leaflet sent ou
contains amongst other matters the following:—*‘ That
deputations should be appointed in each locality to
urge on shopkeepers tke necessity of stocking Irish
goods. That individual members of the Organisation
be requested to give their custom to shopkeepers who
stock Irish goods. That branches be requested to have
displayed in their meeting rooms the names of shop-
keepers and merchants who stock Irish-manufactured

.goods in their districts, and that they instruct tkeir

secretaries to send to every householder in each district
the names of such shopkeepers and merchants as give
preference to goods of Irish manufacture.””

.Coercion Act in force.

. ties proclaimed !

U.LL. will take this matter up seriously. We are not
sanguine enough to expect that all, or even a majority,
will do so, but even if a minority do sometking they
will do much to strengthen the United Irish League in
the estimation of practical men; and it badly needs
such a tonic.

We note that a pamphlet containing a report of a-
speech delivered by Mr. John Redmond on the Finan-
cial Relations question has also been sent to the
branches, and the letter states:—‘‘ In this matter also
it is of the greatest possible importance that the mem-
bers of your branchk should be instructed on the con-
scquences attaching to the over-taxation of Ireland,
and the resultant impoverishment of.our people. The
incidence of taxation should be pointed out to our
people, and steps taken to promote great public meet-
ings in convenient centres, in order to afford oppor-
tunity for an emphatic expression of opinion on the
injustice under which Ireland suffers in this matter.””
One of the best ways, it seems to us, of forcing the
taxation question to the front is by one or more persons
refusing to pay their income tax on principle. The
income tax is just tke tax that could be defied with
dramatic effect. Even one man refusing to pay on
principle until the whole question of the financial re-
lations between England and Ireland was tackled would
at once drive the overtaxation question to the fore-
front. If the man’s furniture was seized all the better,
if he was put in jail even better still.' Probably some
United Irish Leaguers will step into the breach. We
have seen that Mr. John Sweetman doesn’t propose to
risk his furniture being seized, and so does not intend
to passively resist the Britisk: Income Tax gatherer;
Mr. Edward Martyn has not yet declared that he loves
his furniture better than his fight, but we take it that
his silence means that it will be left to United Irish
Leaguers, so far as he is concerned, to defy the British
Taxgatherers.

We see that Mr. Seumas MacManus attempts to de-
fend Mr. John Sweetman for preferring his furniture
to his fight. His argument appears to be that Mr.
Sweetman is quite entitled to fight according to his
own judgment and not rush at the enemy where his
critics would have him. Quite so. But where is Mr.
Sweetman fighting the enemy? We have asked him
why does he not refuse to pay his Income Tax. Mr.
MacManus replies by way of defending him that Mr.
Sweetman is entitled to order his fight in his own way.
Boit so. Then.in what way does Mr. Sweetman intend
to fight? Having decided mot to say boo to the In-
come Tax gatherer, does he intend to go -cattle-
driving? He is a Vice-President of a loud talking
coterie, so that he at least is bound to give some lead
in the fighting way and make some sacrifices.

What is cattle-driving going to lead to? Perhaps
scmeone who has thought out tke cattle-driving policy
would let us have his views on the matter. We con-
fess we are-somewhat in the dark ourselves. Naturally
our sympathies are with the cattle-drivers, but the
question is not a matter of sympathy but a matter of
cool judgment. . What, from the national point of
view, is to be gained or lost by it? We see that the
precious ‘‘ Liberal >’ Government has proclaimed six
counties, and by a juggle that does more credit to its
trickery than its honesty it refrains from putting the
Under the ‘‘ Liberal >’ Govern-
‘“ coercion,’’ there are only six coun-
The six counties, we take it, will be
saddled with the cost of the extra police.

The performance that disgusted EdwaTd VII. must 
have been as bad or worse than tl:'e performance at the 
Lyric, the music-hall which we closed up, and which we 
found the " Irish Times " and the " Freeman " puffing. 
King Edward VII., it appears, left the theatre as a 
protest. 
As soon as the Royal Box was empty we read 
that " all the English and Americans in the theatre 
rose, and walked out." 
Why didn't they walk out before 
Edward VII.? If Edward VII. sat out the perform-
ance, we suppose the E'nglish and Americans would have 
voted the " horrible " and " app,alling " performance 
a first-class affair r Now that Edward VII. is develop-
ing in this direction, perhaps b,e would come over to the 
Gaiety and to the Royal in Dirty Dublin, and walk out

or ratl:-er we should say a section of the females, in

Perhaps t-he next time " Sapho " comes here Edward

In these days' of democracy, or talk of it, "great" 
conventions within closed doors tickle our sense of the 
ridiculous. 
The "Hungarians" held their convention 
• in private last week-tl:·e "Hungarian" exotic is a 
tender plant in Ireland, and the Irish autumn wind _of 
publicity might wither it. This private and confidential 
ccnvention of portion of the Irish race at home and 
abroad sent out an official report. The " Hungarians," 
i!l direct violation of one of their own showy rules, re-
cently welcomed a Justice of the Peace amidst salvos of 
cheers. 
There is no explanation in the official report 
about that little affair. 
TJ:-e new Executive of the 
"Hungarians" have been directed to do a few things 
that will take some doing. 
One of them is to establish 
a National University "on lines similar to those adopted 
by the peoples of Hungary and Poland!" 
They were 
also directed to establish a daily paper I That is only 
a small matter, a mere detail, and no doubt the Execu-
tive will tackle this easy job first! 
No lack of budding 
oc!itors ready to take on the work will hinder them in

Well, one of the Vice-Presid~nts, Mr.

Perhaps he could-and perhaps he cou.ldn't. 
The Editor of this paper commenced learning Irish 
about nine years ago, he went at it very hard for a while 
a.~yway, he spent several holidays in Irish-speaking dis-
tricts before he started the LEADER, he joined several 
classes from time to time, and as a result he knows a 
litt~e Irish, though probably he will never pass for a 
native speaker; and if he had a special aptitude for 
languages, whicl;i unfortunately he has not, he would 
have. more result to show for his quite considerable ex-
penditure of energy. 
Whether or not Mr. Stephen 
Gwynn, a rather recent 'vert to nationality, has more to 
show as a result of his " very scanty smattering, quite 
recently acquired," we ca.nuot say, and the matter is 
of little interest to us .and of none to the public. 
In the article by Mr. Gwynn to which we refer he/ 
brackets us with some of the "Hungarians," and he 
does it in such a way that the casual reader would take 
it that we were members of the "Green Hungarian 
~and." This is not, to say the least of it, ingenuous. 
'\Vl·en Mr. Stephen Gwynn had probably a sneaking 
rega,rd for t:he "Hungarians " ours was about the only 
Nationalist voice in Ireland that fearlessly 
and 
vigorously tackled these erring, if to a considerable ex-
tent well-meaning, gentlemen. 
This rather new 'vert to Nationalist views, one of 
whose chief claims, if not his chief claim, on the Galway 
el~ctorate was that he was the grandson of Smith 
O'Brien-a claim equally shared by the Mr. Gwynn, of 
Trinity, who was recently on a mission ta.ttling to the 
Big Brother in the interests of Queen Bess's English 
and souper University in Ireland-is a v·ery "superior" 
person. 
He is one of those who has "stepped out of 
his class." 
We doubt if he will not do more in the way 
. of weakening than in the way of strengthening the Par-
. liamentary Party. With execrable taste he set forth 
in a recent book of his that a near relative of an Irish 
Catholic Archbishop was in menial service in his father's 
house. 
As a contributor in the LEADER remarked-" It 
is quite possible that there is a servant in the house of 
some Catholic prelate who holds her head high because 
she is a near relative of Mr. Gwynn, M.P.; but if that

One would expect that such a "superior" and highly

a near relative of an " Idolatrous " Archbishop-would 
not densely confound the " Hungarian '•' coterie with 
the motto of Sinn Fein, which Che "Hungarians" have 
endeavoured to run away with. 
So~e of the Parlia-
mentarians make the claim that they stand for Sinn 
Fein far more than do some of the " Hungarian " 
talkers, and we think they make the claim with justice. 
'\iVhy, every one who claims to be a man of spirit, claims 
that he plays the game of life, private and public, off 
his own bat-in other words, that his motto is self-
reliauce. 
Mr. Gwynn 'asks, and the question is put so as to 
include the Editor of this paper, " what are their proofs 
of devotion '' to the language 1 
That is a personal 
question-for us indeed it is more, it suggests an en-
quiry into our family affairs that would scarcely call for 
a public answer. 
Yet Mr. Gwynn, with that good taste 
which we might expect from the son of the employer of 
a, menial who was a near relative of an Irish "Idola-
trous" Archbishop, publicly asks the question. If Mr. 
Gwynn is anxious for an answer, if he calls round to our 
offices we shall be glad to relate to him the story of the 
"proof of devotion " which the Editor of this paper 
gave to the Irish language with regard to himself, and, 
perhaps what is of more consequence, with regard to his 
family . 
Is Mr. Gwynn a United Irish Leaguer first and a 
Gaelic Leaguer afterwards 1 He is a new recruit to the 
Parli~mentary Party-and the rawer. they are the more 
sometimes they approach to infallibility- and he is also 
a. member-a country member from Raheny on the 
Howth tram line, where they pick cockles on the strand 
--of the Executive of the Gaelic Lea.gue. 
Mr. Gwynn 
says that " it is natural for members of Parliament to 
feel some dislike of an organisation whose influence is 
thrown against them-as I assert that the influence of 
the Gaelic League, generally, is thrown against them-

g wfays prompt to ask assistance of members of Parlia-
‘ment in their Parliamentary capacity. The League is
‘wise to ask that help, for, to my certain knowledge,
without Parliamentary pressure the fees for Irish teach-
ing would not have been recovered, and those who know
anything of the League’s working will value what that
means to the movement. But the League is not wise to
allow its members to antagonise what is infinitely the
most numerous and most powerful group in Irish life.”

We have it here, on the authority of a mcmber of the
Executive of the Gaelic League, that the influence of
that League generally is thrown against members of
Parliament. _ Personally we doubt the accuracy of the
statement, but let that pass. Now it is well over two
years ago since we stepped into the breach in the best
interests of the Gaelic League and protested against the
official organ of that body unduly flirting with what it
called its ‘‘ confrere,”” the United Irishman (since de-
funct). Where were the voice and pen of Mr. Stephen
Gwynn then? Perhaps Mr. Gwynn was rather a
‘ Hungarian > himself at that time and did not sym-
pathise with our protest ?

Mr. Stephen Gwynn’s challenge to Mr. Martyn, Mr.
Sweetman, and others to desperate combat in Irish sug-
gests quite interesting and humorous possibilities. A
hundred years ago the choice was, we believe, usually
between pistols and swords; to-morrow, if Mr. Gwynn’s
example takes on, it may be between the O’Growney
books and Cea¢ta Deaga! If we challenged the editor
of the “ Freeman ’’ to mortal combat in Irish, we feel
sure tkat Mr. Brayden would elect to fight with the
‘“sixth * book of O’Growney! Picture Mr. Edward
Martyn stealing out in the light of the first grey streaks
of dawn to the Nine Acres, Mr. Arthur Griffith, his
second, in his company carrying O’Growney, Book I.,
to meet Mr. Stephen Gwynn, accompanied by Mr. Swift
McNeill. We see the desperate two in our mind’s eye
as they approach each other after the parley between
the seconds is over, and then the hush of the morning
is disturbed by loud warlike cries of “'T4 mé Feap” and
“Tdch apal!”  Away further north in some lonely
nook in the midst of a Meath prairie, from which the
cattle had been scattered the night before, we see Mr.
John Sweetman thrusting b6 agur ct into the prostrate
ferm of Mr. Ginnell, M.P.!

It has occurred to us in view of tke muddle in which
Nationalist politics have got, that one great want in
Ireland is a platform for social intercourse now and
again amongst Irish politicians as such. Why does not
the United Irish League hold a social reception, or
sccial function of some kind or another, say, at the
Mansion House? The evening on which an All-Ire-
land Convention is held would be the best time perhaps,
but at any time such a gathering could be Leld on a
local scale. The Young Ireland Branch of the United
Irish League, for instance, could arrange such a func-
tion on a small scale any time. There should be no
speeches, only conversation between such as liked to
converse, and perhaps a few Irish songs, and attend-
ance at the function should not be taken as implying
adherence to any particular man or section of politi-
cians. The invitations could be confined to Nationa-
lists, or they might even be extended to particular indi-
viduals suck: as Mr. Sloan, Captain Shawe-Taylor, Mr.

THE LEADER.

SEPTEMBER 7, 1907.

Talbot-Crosbie, and such prominent men who claim to
have the interests of the country at heart, even though
they may not be classed as Nationalists. They might
be converted partially or wholly at such gatherings,
and it is not likely that they would convert: the gather-
ing. A reception in the Mansion House, organised by
a Nationalist political body, would not cost very muck,
and if the cost stood in the way the difficulty could be
got over by charging, as at the last Oireachtas recep-
tion, a fee of 2s. 6d. to go in. This was done at the
last Oireachtas—delegates to the Apo e were in-
vited guests—and we believe it worked very well.

We don’t know that such a suggestion has ever been
made before, and it may be worth considering. There
is too much of the water-tight compartment arrange-
ment about the various sections of Irisk Nationalists.
They go round in exclusive masses, never meeting
except perhaps to split one another’s heads, call one
another names, or cut up one another’s political reputa-
tion. The business of a political social gathering would
be to talk politics in the conversational way to those
one might meet at it. Conferences of political leaders
don’t serve the purpose that a social gathering of all
classes and ranks of Nationalist politicians would serve.
A conference between leaders involves the discussing of
a programme, and has a definite end in view. It may
as likely lead to fresh divisions as to mutual understand-
ing. A social gatkering such as we suggest would have
no programme, and no definite objective. ~ Men who
take an interest in politics meet at the restaurants, at
drinking bars, at swimming baths, at the Forty Foot
Hole, and other places, but they don’t meet as politi-
cians, and it is only by chance they may talk politics.
At public meetings only those who agree with the object
of the particular meeting attead—sometimes a few ill-
disposed persons go with tin cans to drown the speeches
—and these former attend as units, not knowing one
another, and disperse.
meeting held in the Mansion House on Wednesday night
of this week. Speeches will have been delivered there;
but that is not a political social gathering. Are there
not a great deal too many speeches and too little inter-
course between politicians, both leaders and followers?
We think such meetings as we suggest would make for
political health, stability and tolerance.

Timid Catholics may not approve of tle exposure of
the anti-Catholic bigotry vividly illustrated in the fol-
lowing statement of the disappointment of a poor young
Catholic boy :—Henry Murray, a lad of about 16 years
of age, of 16 Richmond Hill, Rathmines, presented him-
self in reply to an advertisement in the ‘“ Irish Times ’’
over the name of Mr. Shanks for a post of Cashier in
the International Exhibition. The poor young lad, a
smart, intelligent boy, was not examined as to his effi-
ciency or character, but was simply asked wkere was
he educated, and what was his religion, and on replying
that he was a Catholic, and educated by the Christint
Brothers, he was told he would not suit, and was cu*
dismissed. If Mr. Skanks and the Committee of u:::
International Exhibition have any answer to this state-
ment of poor Henry Murray we shall be pleased to pub-
lisk: it. Three other boys to his knowledge were re-
jected on the same grounds of bigotry. Of course, in
point of fact the rejected Catholics may number 103 for
all we know. .

The Practical Series' Co.,”” Athboy and Dublin,
publish a number of school copy-books that are not only
printed in Ireland, but are printed on Irish paper. We
ha.ve. a few samples before us. A drawing-book,
specially designed for use in National Schools and
approved by the Commissioners, appears to us very
good value at its price—one penny; also a penny exer-
cise book likewise designed and approved for National
Schools; whilst an exercise book of 100 pages, ruled and
made up of Ballyclare paper, appears to us a marvel
of cheapness at 1d. The Maynooth exercise book, price
twopence, and the Ophthalmic exercise book, one
penny, which was exhibited at the Sanitary Congress in
London, are both excellent specimens of Irish manufac-
turers’ work. The Practical Series Co., besides other
publications, have a line of Irish vellum notepaper
manufactured specially for them at Ballyclare.

A correspondent sends us a copy of the English
“ Catholic Times’’ which contains tke following adver-
tisement :—‘‘ Smart, Active Man, Roman Catholic, Un-
married; experienced in infirmary work and care of
sick ; skilled in ordinary tailoring repairs and the use

Training College, Waterford.””  Our correspondent
writes on the copy of the British paper he sends us:—
““ Could no Irishman be got to fill this post? Perhaps
the Principal is an Englishman.””  We don’t know what
the Principal may be in the way of nationality. We
see tkat a pensioner is preferred. There is a story told
of the late Father Healy, of Little Bray. He was din-
ing out one evening, and a scientist—we think it was
Professor Huxley—remarked that a Catholic priest once
asked him pointedly if he had ever discovered traces of
intelligence in an ape. ‘Now, what could have in-
duced him to ask that question,”” said he to Father
Healy. ““Ah, I suppose,’” replied Father Healy, ““ he
was on {Le look-out for a chape curate.”” And so, in
niany instances, we have ‘‘ a pensioner preferred.” But
why must he be a ‘ Roman Catholic?” Would not a
plain, honest Catholic be good enough for the De La
Salle Training College, Waterford? By stipulating in
an English paper for a ‘“ Roman Catholic”’ and a pen-
sicner for choice, does the College harbour the delight-
ful hope that it may coax a real live *“ dump ’’ from the
white skores of Albion?

The important question of standardising samples of
goods for Irish public bodies was considered at a con-
ference of representatives of public bodies in Ireland
one day last week. Mr. Kelly, of Edenderry, sa{d there
was generally complete agreement with the objects of
the conference, but there seemed to be a want of know-
ledge in some cases where the public bodies appeared to
be humbugged by contractors or manufacturers who led
them to believe that certain articles were made in Ire-
Jand which were not made here, and also that certain
geods whick: could actually be got in Ireland were not
obtainable here. There was a lengthy conference, and
a small committee was appointed to interview the heads
of large firms so as to obtain their co-operation and the
advice of their buyers in this way, and it was decided
that the officials of various public bodies be asked to
assist them.

The annual Waterford Feis was held in the Court-
house grounds on Sunday week with the favourable cir-
cumstances of good weatker and a large attendance.
Father Furlong, President of the Waterford Branch of
Connpdo na Jaevilge, opened the proceedings. We
gather from the opening speech of Father Furlong that
some of the local schools are not in a furious hurry to
turn out their pupils Irish. Commenting on the teach-
ing of Irish history in these sckools, Father Furlong
said:—“In the first place he had to express his dis-
appointment that some of the city schools had not sent
pupils to compete in some of the subjects, such as
elementary history, Gaelic art, and so on. One school
stood out, however, pre-eminently as an exception, and
it was only fair to give it prominence. He referred to
the Ursuline National School. It was more than dis-
appointing tkat when so many and strenuous efforts
were being made to.revive the national language there
had been so little support coming from the schools.
Indeed one might think on looking over the programme
that Irish history was not taught in their schools at
all, with the one praiseworthy exception to wkich he
had referred. Supposing such instruction were excluded
from the primary schools curriculum by Government
enactment the whole country would be up in arms.to
agitate against such an injustice.”’ Father Furlong’s
outspoken criticism of this lamentable state of affairs
will, we trust, awaken a little.more Irish spirit in the
school authorities responsible for it.

afforded in the columns of our contemporary should be
useful and interesting to the general cyclist.

Dear Sir—I beg to inform you that tho annual Celtic Congress
of the Union Regionaliste Bretonne took place at Rostrenen,
from the 9th to the 15th of August, and that it met the greatest
success. The section for the Preservation of the Breton Language
had the satisfaction to note that every year a greater quantity of
books, reviews and papers are printed out in Breton language;
and the competitions were attended by many poets and prose-
writers. The Sections for History and Beaux-Arts received many
interesting contributions.  The Economical 'Section obtained a
great success, its work during the present year will include
especially—1. The preparation of an economical geography of
Brittany; 2. The study of a plan for the project of a transatlantic
harbour on the shore of Brittany. -
It was a pleasure for me to remark that the example of Ireland
was always before our minds; the discussion for a Breton trade-
mark was inspired by your Irish trade-mark, and the organisation
of the Gaelic League and of your national works were also set as
examples by us. The sympathy for the other Celtic countries is—
as you know—a very living one in our old Brittany, and a mention of
Erin met always loud applauses. During the musical performances
but one exception was made to the rule of singing in Breton lan-
guage only, and it was in favour of the ‘Chanson des Gars
d’Irlande,” by Augusta Holmes.
Brittany and Ireland for ever! Breiz hag Iverzon da virviken!
Believe me, Dear Sir,
Very faithfully yours,

NE Dublin Horse Show is very much the same

as another, yet, like the Christmas season, as

they only come once a year, one has a welcome for them
every time. An unsophisticated person from some
backward rural part would probably imagine Leaps of
unfounded things about the well-dressed, loud-talking
and grinning throng in the jumping enclosure; he
might, for instance, conclude that they were all ladies
and gentlemen, that they were mnot only solvent, but
wealthy, that they had paid their tailors’ and dress-
makers’ bills, and that they were all intelligent and

i

least as interestingly as the charac-
have the smart dialogue parts in the
comedies. We are not unsophisticated
people from a backward part of the country, so

, We every year enjoy a half cynical but good-natured

Lour or two turning that gay and giddy throng over in
our minds. Were we fanatics we might scowl at them;
.hoyvever, we make open confession that we heartily
enjoy an hour or two once in the year observing them
-and listening to their prattle.

They wear their faces largely on the grin—that ap-
pears to be the fashionable way of wearing the face at
~the Horse Show. The difference between us and the
giddy people at a Horse Show is that we only laugh
when we are amused, but they can keep up a grin
wlen they are utterly miserable and disappointed. We
.could not laugh with them, but we find it no difficult
task at all to laugh at them.

What is called ““Society ”’ in Anglo-Ireland is from
its very nature apish; it is always looking up to men
and things who at least ought to be looked at on the
level if not looked down upon. It stands glaring,
vulgar, and inquisitive before the Royal box, or what-
ever they call 1t, where the Lord Lieutenant and his
friends and flunkeys gather—like a servant-maid ex-
amining the wardrobe of lLer mistress. The Lord
Lieutenant, a human cork on a seething human sea, one
time driven into the Oireachtas, now proclaiming six
counties, sits there earning his twenty thousand a year,
or whatever he gets for his job, and the males and
females of the swell mob in the enclosure stare up at
him and his friends and flunkies as if they were a new
species of mounkey on show at tke Zoo. = “ Which is
Lady This”’? “Oh, doesn’t Lady That look simply
sweet in blue?”’ and so amid a fusilade of such profound
remarks, the well-dressed servant maids and servant
boys of ‘“Society” glare up at the Vice-regal
menagerie. One human document bearing the uuter
semblance of a man asked a particularly stupid-looking
policeman what was the name of a particular person
in the Royal box. ‘I don’t know, then,’”” said Mr.
Bobby. ““Aren’t you stationed here?’ retorted
the thing with the outer semblance of a man. I
am,”’” said Mr. Bobby, stolidly, and in such a way that
we concluded that Mr. Bobby was not by any means
such a fool as he looked.

The sparkling dialogue that bubbles like intellectual
champagne all over the fashionable enclosure, exists
only in the fancy of the unsopListicated on-looker from
the backward parts of the country. What flat dialogue
it all is, retailed, though it often be, in a loud voice,
s> that the neighbours can hear it all.

IN 5‘1’1“9_0f everything, in spite even of the croakers,
= W0 still continue to get, ahead ; we are still having

Sheehan’s book, An t-Athair Peadar’s booi{, and
Sceiligh’s boqk—eacl'.' a rather big piece of work—came
out almost simultaneously, a feat whick: only a short
time since would have seemed impossible in Ireland.
Now, T have in my hands the first two of a series of a
half-dozen arrangements of Irish airs designed to suit
the ordinary Gaelic League choir. The harmonisation
is done by Mr. P. J. O’Sullivan, whose name has been
for many years familiar to Ireland in connection with
Irish music. I think these arrangements will serve to
greatly enhance his reputation. Anyone who has had
anything to do with Gaelic League choirs knows the
difficulty of obtaining harmonised Irish airs. Anglo-
Irish airs, ‘“Moore’s Melodies,”” were to be had in
legions; it was not so with the airs which tke Gaelic
League required. And those that were to be had were
no great joy. The only Gaelic League chorus which I
felt at home in, and I have been singing many of them,
was the ‘ Rallying Song *’—a fine chorus, out and out.
These arrangements of Mr. O’Sullivan’s come in beauti-
fully to supply the want; in future no Gaelic League
choir need be gavelled for lack of matter. It would
not do to judge these arrangements off-hand by aid of
the ordinary laws of harmony. Mr. O’Sullivan has cer-
tain definite ideas regarding the harmonisation of Irish
melodies; these he has again and again explained in
various places ; and these he consistently maintains when
he sets out to do the work himself. For instance, he
holds that accidentals must not be used. Now try to
write an imitation passage without using accidentals.
You find tkat the only imitation which serves is imita-
tion of the octave. This is habitually used by the
arranger, and with good effect. ~ Other rules too he
holds’ with ; the result is to preserve a flavour, a blas,
which lets the observing audience know that this is not
German nor Italian nor English music which is being
performed. Thus are preserved the distinctive charac-
teristics of the melodies themselves. The first of the
two already published, ‘ Ceol an Mhala,” is a fine
sprightly air. It is simply arranged; any orfimary
Gaelic League choir should be able to do it. Tt is very
singable; almost every part is melodious. The words
are by Torna. The arranger has added a pretty coda
which puts a badly-needed finish on the melody. ~May
T add that it is not one of the melodies of wkich we
are all tired to death. The second of the series, ‘ Slan
le Maig,”” is more ambitiously arranged. Its well-doing
would require some well-trained tenor, and they are not
easily had. But it would, T think, repay careful
handling. It is, in my opinion, one of the most perfect

THE NATIONAL THEATRE SOCIETY IN CORK.

AM not a dramatic critic, nor even a frequenter of
playhouses. The jugglers of the Palace and the
musical comedians of the Opera House are alike un-
attractive to me. This perkaps may account for the
way in which the plays of the National Theatre Society
appealed to me last week—my unsophisticated taste
relishing fare at which the epicurean critic would turn
up his nose. Be that as it may, the performances gave
me unalloyed pleasure. It makes me quite envious to
think how long Dublin has enjoyed this treat all to
itself. I didn’t quite know what to expect wken I went
to see the Abbey players. I had hazy impressions of
visits in days gone by to plays like ‘ Robert Emmet,”’
““ Con the Shaughraun,”’ and the ‘“ Colleen Bawn,”’ as
staged by such strolling barnstormers as chanced to our
little town. The bright eye of youth saw no defects
there, and I have never wished to destroy those illu-
sions by going to see with the more discerning eye of a
maturer age. Hence it was that my feelings were rather
mixed when I found myself in the Opera House on a
recent Wednesday night. There was a scanty attendance
that night; on the following nights things improved a
little. While waiting for the curtain to rise we were
entertained by the orchestra with—selections from I
kuow not what musical comedies! The Cork audience
beat time with their feet, and applauded loudly. And
Corkonians are famed for tLeir keen sense of the ludi-
crous; the man who spread that story was surely a
satirist. 5
But with the rise of the curtain I forgot all that—
fcrgot Cork and its incongruities. In a moment I
knew I should not be disappointed. My boyish illu-
sions were replaced by realities sweeter than the illu-
sions. FHere I saw men and women that I knew, and
heard talk that was not strange to me: homely talk of
marriage and bargains and writs and farms of land.

Tere I saw before me the life of my own people, and it
was not caricatured nor demeaned to a laugking-stock. -
I was at home, and felt delighted and happy. And
ever since I’ve been seeing plays in the'streets a.nd
market-places, where formerly I passec} with unseeing
eyes. ’Tis good to be shown these things, and I am
grateful to the Abbey players. I cannot now pass a
% hooded-cloak >’ woman in the streets without think-
ing of poor Kathleen Ni Houlihan, with her ragged
cloak and worn face. The girls witk: their shawls and
uncovered heads, the rough-coated farmers, t‘he deaf
apple-woman, and the tatterdemalion ballad-singer—I
meet them every day in Cork.

Taking the plays together, what appealed to me most
was their restraint. There is power in nearly all of
them ; the dramatic action is well sustained throughout,
and the catastrophe, in the Greek sense of tke ‘\vord,
always immediately precedes the fall of the curtain. 1
can find no words of praise sufficient for the actors.
They are so true, so unaffected, and they put their
hearts so much into their work. An almost empty
hcuse doesn’t seem to discourage them in the least.
The delivery and elocution were all tlkat could be
desired. And the accents! W. G. Fay is a marvel.
Michael Cooney’s broad Limerick brogue, with its in-
tonation running up and down the scale, was music to
me. What can I say of Arthur Sinclair in the ‘‘ Ris-
ing of the Moon?’ I would travel fifty miles to hear
the Sergeant show the ballad-singer how ¢ Granu Aile’’
should be sung.

I intended to say a word about tke plays individually,
but I’'m afraid each would take an article to itself. Of
the comedies I most enjoyed ‘‘ The Jackdaw ;" perhaps
because I am not a critic. I laughed heartily at it.
Cooney struck me as an ordinary type of Irish farmer,
shrewd, close-fisted, a hard man at a bargain, yet with
a soft spot in his heart after all. The Widow Broderick,
witk: her check apron and black bonnet, is an old friend ;
many a time I have been in her little huckster’s shop.
‘“ Hyacinth Halvey ”’ did not please me as much as
‘“ Spreading the News.”” Hyacinth, with his burden-
scme ‘‘ charackter,”” was too broad for my taste. The
postmistress was excellent; it could hardly be other-
wise with Sara Allgood filling the part. Brigit
O’Dempsey exacted all the respect due to the parish
priest’s Lousekeeper. ‘‘ The Rising of the Moon’’ was
great. I have a respect for the Force now, and feel
inclined to salute when I see the stripes on a sergeant’s
cuff. ‘“The Hour Glass’’ rather puzzles me; I’'m not

clear as to what I think about it, but of one point I'm -

certain, Ernest Vaughan’s elocution is beyond all
praise.

BLACKROCK COLLEGE, C.8.5p., BLACKROCK.

BOARDERS return on 2nd September. DAY SCHOLARS
must be present at 10 a.m. on 3rd, when classes aro resumed.

By its long and brilliant record of success, Blackrock has shown
that it affords it students the highest educational advantages;
while its beautiful grounds, buildings and position, just overlook-
ing Dublin Bay, give it a combination of attractions which are
quite unique. Last year, in the Intermediate Exams., it obtained
18 Exhibitions, 3 Senior, 7 Middle, 8 Junior, 33 distinctions, 131

passes.
14 SCHOLARSHIPS (£350) to be competed for on 30th August.

THE URIVERSITY & CIVIL SERVICE COLLEGE,]

A distinct institution, and the only residential College of its kind
in Ireland, affords young men an opportunity of receiving a higher
University and Civil Service training under proper religious safe-
guards and away from the dangerous seductions of city life. In
addition to its well-known University successes, Blackrock has
obtained this year:—Indian Civil Service, Jas. Sweeney; Indian
Police, Jn. Slattery; Nat. School Inspectorship, J. Doody; Army
Accounts, MI. Flanagan; Exchequer Dept., T. Casey; Sandhurst,
G. C. Hughes; II. Division, M. Kinane. FOUR SCHOLAR-
SHIPS (£100) to bo awarded in September.
